From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

US record chart published by Billboard

Not to be confused with [Hot R&B Songs](/wiki/Hot_R%26B_Songs "Hot R&B Songs").

The **Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs** [chart](/wiki/Record_chart "Record chart") ranks the most popular [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B") and [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") songs in the United States and is published weekly by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")*. Rankings are based on a measure of radio airplay, sales data, and streaming activity.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The chart had 100 positions but was shortened to 50 positions in October 2012.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The chart is used to track the success of [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") songs in [urban](/wiki/Urban_contemporary_music "Urban contemporary music"), or primarily [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American"), venues. Dominated over the years at various times by [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [Blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), [doo-wop](/wiki/Doo-wop "Doo-wop"), [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), and [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), it is today dominated by [contemporary R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B") and hip hop. Since its inception, the chart has changed its name many times in order to accurately reflect the industry at the time.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hot_R%26B/Hip-Hop_Songs&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Beginning in 1942, *Billboard* published a chart of bestselling [African-American music](/wiki/African-American_music "African-American music"), first as the **Harlem Hit Parade**, then as **Race Records**. Then in 1949, *Billboard* began publishing a **Rhythm and Blues** chart, which entered "R&B" into mainstream lexicon.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanneh202191-4) These three charts were consolidated into a single **Hot R&B Singles** chart in October 1958.

From November 30, 1963, to January 23, 1965, there were no *Billboard* R&B singles charts.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitburn1996xiii-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanneh202187–88-6) The "Hot R&B Singles" chart was discontinued when *Billboard* determined it unnecessary due to so much crossover of titles between the R&B and pop charts in light of the rise of [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown"). The chart was reinstated as **Hot Rhythm & Blues Singles** on January 30, 1965.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitburn1996xiv-7)

Beginning August 23, 1969,
the chart was renamed to **Best Selling Soul Singles**. The move was made by a *Billboard* editorial decision that the term "soul" more accurately accounted for the "broad range of song and instrumental material which derives from the musical genius of the black American".[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanneh202195-9) Beginning on July 14, 1973, the chart title was modified slightly to **Hot Soul Singles**. In late June 1982, the chart was renamed again, this time to **Hot Black Singles** because the music that African-Americans were buying and listening to had a "greater stylistic variety than the soul sound" of the early 1970s. Black Singles was deemed an acceptable term to encompass pop, funk, and early rap music popular in urban communities.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Beginning October 27, 1990, the Hot Black Singles chart was returned to the Hot R&B Singles name first used in 1958.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitburn1996xii-11) In the December 5, 1992, issue of *Billboard*, a new [Bubbling Under Hot R&B Singles](/w/index.php?title=Bubbling_Under_Hot_R%26B_Singles&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bubbling Under Hot R&B Singles (page does not exist)") chart was introduced, modeled on the [Bubbling Under Hot 100](/wiki/Bubbling_Under_Hot_100 "Bubbling Under Hot 100").[[12]](#cite_note-Ellis-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Hip hop was introduced to the chart beginning with the December 11, 1999 issue, when *Billboard* changed the name to **Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Singles & Tracks** to recognize the influence and relationship of [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") to the genre.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Within a few years, the crossover of R&B titles onto the pop chart was so significant that all Top Ten songs on the *Billboard* Hot 100 chart on October 11, 2003, were by black artists.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The lengthy title was shortened to **Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs** on April 30, 2005.

The chart's methodology was changed starting with the October 20, 2012 issue, to match the *Billboard* Hot 100's---incorporating digital downloads and video streaming data (R&B/Hip-Hop Digital Songs) and combining it with airplay of R&B and hip-hop songs across all radio formats, to determine song position. Also at this time, the chart was shortened to 50 positions.

| June 1949 – October 1958 | Rhythm & Blues Records *(two or three separate charts—see above)* |
| October 1958 – October 1962[[16]](#cite_note-16) | Hot R&B Sides |

| November 1963 – January 1965[[19]](#cite_note-:0-19) | *No chart published (see above)* |
| January 1965 – August 1969 | Hot Rhythm & Blues Singles |
| August 1969 - July 1973 | Best Selling Soul Singles |
| July 1973 - June 1982 | Hot Soul Singles |
| June 1982 – October 1990 | Hot Black Singles |
| October 1990 – January 1999 | Hot R&B Singles |
| January – December 1999 | Hot R&B Singles & Tracks |
| December 1999 – April 2005 | Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Singles & Tracks |
| April 2005 – present | Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs |

## Bubbling Under R&B/Hip-Hop Songs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hot_R%26B/Hip-Hop_Songs&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Bubbling Under R&B/Hip-Hop Songs")]

Bubbling Under R&B/Hip-Hop Songs[[71]](#cite_note-71) was a chart composed of 25 positions that represented songs making progress to chart on the main R&B/Hip-Hop Songs chart. Many times, songs halted their progress at this chart and never debuted on the Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs chart. The Bubbling Under R&B/Hip-Hop Songs chart could have also been seen as a 25 position quasi-addendum to the chart, since the chart represented the 25 songs below position number 50 that had not previously appeared on the main chart.
